author: @InformationEngineer59
text: Interesting, intriguing!  A CVT without belts.  There is an idiosyncrasy in this design that isn't very obvious -- the speed of the output is not constant, as the input turns around, one at a time each ratcheted gear becomes the drive gear.  But each drive gear is pushing in an arc, not a straight line.  The more gears you have, the smoother the output, but this idiosyncratic output would have to be compensated for.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it does and a spring clutch on the outpput will take care of this - thanks for pointing it out - cheers

author: @hashbrown777
text:
I'd have a look at the swing angle at "max yoke" (haha, 'top gear') and base the number of planets on that. Looks like it might be around 60°, so 6 gears (to make 360)?
No science behind that but Im thinking to make the torque transfer as smooth as possible for the whole travel, so the theres no uneven levers at certain points.
If there's a nice mechanism that allows you to rectify both forward and backward components of the reciprocation that would be an amazing bit to include so opposing levers can share the load at their widest instead of at the moment being unbalanced (on lever transfers all the force and the other just ratchets)

author: @mfree80286
text: 5:20 One could always stack larger planet gears the same way you've stacked the levers. No reason they can't overlap, the sun gear would just need to be longer (and presumably a little stiffer because there's no directly opposing gear contact).

author: @safffff1000
text: Doesn't that design have lots of friction points, especially under load?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - i can see what you think, i think. Because it has levers you are seeing sliding friction - but they only slide when you are changing gear - the rest of time it is rolling friction - so pretty much it sees no more friction than any other geared drive

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Ok

author: @charlesjmouse
text:
Very interesting.
I could imagine something like this also being very useful in the drivetrain of a peddle-assist e-bike, both to simplify the mechanics and reduce the size of  the drive motor ...while you don't need a gearbox for speed adjustment in a sufficiently powerful electric motor, a smaller motor with it's own benefits would appreciate a variable torque multiplier.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cheers mate

author: @st0rmerc00l8
text: I hope for the day that someone invents a CVT that works without friction connections or one way rectifying systems. It appears to be very very difficult to make such a system. Perhaps some kind of 'conservation of angular momentum' cvt that uses flywheels that change in size like a ballerina pulling her arms in; to change torque and speed, would be possible?

replies.author: @martin-vv9lf
replies.text:
I am convinced it is possible to build a cvt that works on the principle of slippage, i.e. the inverse of friction. that way you avoid the damage common to belts and pulleys. 
In most cases, cvt aren't necessary, and in cases where it is necessary to avoid the power loss during gear changes, a second engine and transmission can be used during the intermission.

replies.author: @st0rmerc00l8
replies.text: ​@martin-vv9lf your idea is intriguing, I have been constantly thinking about CVTs, and believe that there are many more practical principles able to be exploited other than rectified reciprocating motion, friction or electrical converters. How would you slipping system work in theory?

replies.author: @martin-vv9lf
replies.text: @st0rmerc00l8 are you familiar with the method in geometry of using a set square to divide a line into equal parts? my belief is that the same sliding set square method can be applied to scale speed, and not just length. although that method relates to a line of finite length, it may be possible to apply the method to a line of infinite length.

replies.author: @HypoceeYT
replies.text:
It is in fact proven impossible to make a smooth-running positive-engagement CVT, in much the same way and kind of for the same mathematical reasons that it's proven impossible to "square the circle" using Euclid's rules.

Now, in practice would a "mere" 1000 different gear ratios with slightly rough motion suffice rather than a truly CV transmission? Well, that's a different question.
